title: When Agentic RAG Is Worth the Complexity
concept: Simple factual lookups don't need it; multi-step research questions do.

exercises.prompt: In the last lesson, we compared standard Pipeline RAG with Agentic RAG. What is the key difference?

options.text: Standard RAG uses agents, while Agentic RAG uses simple databases.
Standard RAG follows a fixed path of retrieve-then-generate, while Agentic RAG dynamically decides when and what to retrieve.
Standard RAG is slower and more expensive than Agentic RAG.

exercises.explain: Standard Pipeline RAG flows in a fixed, linear order (Retrieve then Generate). Agentic RAG uses an LLM loop to dynamically decide what to search, read, and search again.

cards.title: Is It Worth It?
cards.body: Agentic RAG sounds amazing because it lets an AI behave like a researcher. But building, debugging, and running it is expensive and slow. How do you know when you actually need it, and when standard RAG is more than enough?

cards.title: The Cost of Autonomy
cards.body: Agentic RAG adds loops, tool-use, and decision-making to document retrieval. While powerful, it introduces high latency and cost, meaning it should only be used when standard, single-shot search pipelines fail to handle complex, multi-step questions.

cards.title: The Calculator vs. The Adviser
cards.body: Imagine a simple calculator versus a financial adviser. If you just need to add two numbers, a calculator does the job instantly. If you need to plan a retirement strategy based on changing tax laws, you need an active adviser who asks questions and adjusts plans step-by-step.

cards.title: Simple Lookup vs. Research Project
cards.body: If a user asks 'What is our return policy?', standard RAG retrieves the policy document in one step. But if a user asks 'Compare our current return policy to the 2022 policy and list the main changes,' Agentic RAG is needed to plan, retrieve both documents, and compare them.

cards.title: More Power Isn't Always Better
cards.body: You might think that because Agentic RAG is more advanced, you should use it for every search task. In reality, using an agent to find a simple FAQ answer is like hiring a private detective just to look up a phone number in a directory.

cards.title: Matching Tool to Task
cards.body: Choosing the right tool for the job saves time, computing power, and money.

left.text: Ideal for: 'What is the WiFi password?' (One query, immediate answer)

right.text: Ideal for: 'Who set up the WiFi, and why did they change the security protocol last month?' (Requires planning and multiple lookups)

exercises.prompt: Which of the following scenarios is NOT a good fit for Agentic RAG and should use standard RAG instead?

options.text: Finding and comparing the revenue growth of three competitors over five years.
Synthesizing conflicting reports on a project's timeline to find the root delay.
Looking up the office phone number of the HR director.
Searching through customer logs to diagnose why a specific API key failed multiple times last week.

exercises.explain: A simple factual lookup like finding a phone number requires no planning or loops. A single search query to a database is fast, cheap, and sufficient.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Agentic RAG typically has higher latency and cost compared to standard Pipeline RAG.
exercises.explain: Agentic RAG requires multiple LLM calls to plan and decide actions, which takes significantly more time (latency) and costs more in API tokens than a single retrieve-and-generate cycle.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing phrase to complete the rule of thumb for RAG systems:
exercises.sentence: Use standard RAG for direct lookups, but choose Agentic RAG for multi-step research tasks.
exercises.explain: Standard RAG works perfectly for simple, single-document lookups. You only need the complexity of Agentic RAG when you have multi-step research tasks that require planning and synthesis.

exercises.prompt: Which response describes an Agentic RAG pattern rather than a standard RAG pattern?

options.text: A system retrieves the 'Pricing FAQ' document, finds the price of Plan A, and prints it out.
A search engine matches the keywords 'weather Tokyo' and returns the current temperature.
An assistant searches for Company X's CEO, reads that they recently changed, and then launches a second search to find the new CEO's background history.

exercises.explain: Option C shows an agent autonomously planning, fetching data sequentially, and dynamically deciding what to search next based on prior findings. Options A and B are simple, single-turn lookups.

exercises.prompt: When deciding to upgrade a system from standard Pipeline RAG to Agentic RAG, what is the primary trade-off to consider?

options.text: Because it uses fewer LLM calls and runs much faster than standard RAG.
Because it guarantees 100% factual accuracy for basic search queries.
Because the higher latency and cost are only justified when the LLM must plan and execute multi-step research.
Because simple lookup queries require complex decision loops to find the correct database.

exercises.explain: Agentic RAG introduces significant API costs, latency, and points of failure. For simple facts like a store's location, a basic search pipeline is faster and cheaper. Agentic RAG is only worth the complexity when the system must make decisions, run sequential searches, or synthesize contradictory data.
